verifier_values.criteria: Mentions the patient's chief complaint is elevated blood pressure.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
Prompt: "She states she has elevated blood pressure and denies any other review of system symptoms."

As per the chief complaint described in the prompt, the patient comes in with elevated blood pressure, and her blood pressure log (attached) shows that for the last couple of days, she has had elevated blood pressure.

verifier_values.criteria: Mentions that the patient's blood pressure at the last visit was normal.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
According to ACC/AHA guideline, normal is less than 120/80. "Blood pressure is classified by the following framework: normal blood pressure is defined as <120 mm Hg systolic and <80 mm Hg diastolic"
 https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/epub/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001356

Clinical Notes-Progress Notes-Column D-Row 193 shows the last blood pressure reading for this patient was normal at "109/69".

verifier_values.criteria: Mentions that the diagnosis of primary hypertension requires accurate blood pressure measurement.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
"Accurate measurement of BP is essential to diagnose high BP, ascertain BP-related CVD risk, and assess response to therapy. Many errors in BP measurement, which have been examined in 2 systematic reviews, can be avoided by following a standardized protocol that has been a mainstay of clinical trials that include BP measurement". 
According to Figure 3 (Checklist for Accurate Office Blood Pressure Measurement), an accurate office blood pressure measurement first needs to be taken in order to see if this corresponds with at-home readings. https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/epub/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001356

verifier_values.criteria: Mentions it is necessary to verify that blood pressure is being measured correctly.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
"A standardized protocol, provided in Figure 3, includes proper patient preparation, standardized measurement technique and approach, documentation of BP, analysis of the readings, and providing the readings to the patient. The use of a BP device validated against a reference standard with an appropriately sized cuff is paramount for accurate BP measurement (see https://www.validatebp.org for a carefully vetted list of validated devices available in the United States). Further, because individual BP measurements may vary in an unpredictable or random manner, a single reading is inadequate for clinical decision-making. Office BP should be based on the average of available readings, and an average of ≥2 BP measurements obtained on ≥2 separate occasions may minimize error and provide a more accurate estimation of office BP."

Figure 3, ACC/AHA guidelines for Blood pressure management 
https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/epub/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001356

verifier_values.criteria: Mentions the patient's home blood pressure readings have only been taken in the morning hours.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
It is important to recognize that the patient only takes her blood pressure in the morning hours. Identifying the early morning BP surge pattern could allow providers to refrain from starting medications given hormonal patterns. 

Per Patient Images.pdf: 
"Date  Time (a.m.)
01/01/2026 8:15 
01/02/2026 8:20
01/03/2026 8:30
01/04/2026 8:00"

"Hypertension screening and management, including the use of BP targets, have primarily relied on BP measured in the office setting. Out-of-office measurement of BP using ABPM or HBPM, which is also known as self-measurement of BP at home, can provide valuable information beyond office BP for the confirmation and management of hypertension. Both ABPM and HBPM provide BP estimates that are based on a greater number of BP measurements than are obtained in the office setting, enhancing the accuracy and precision for detecting a patient’s true and usual BP levels. ABPM typically involves wearing a fully automated device, usually over a period of 24 hours, with out-of-office BP readings obtained at relatively frequent intervals during the daytime (ie, 15-30 minutes) and less frequent intervals at nighttime (ie, 30-60 minutes). ABPM can: 1) provide estimates of mean BP over the entire monitoring period, and separately during daytime and nighttime; 2) determine the daytime-to-nighttime BP ratio to identify the extent of nocturnal “dipping”; 3) identify the early-morning BP surge pattern; 4) estimate BP variability; and 5) allow for recognition of hypotension. HBPM involves the patient measuring their BP at home using an oscillometric device over days to weeks, which can provide estimates of mean BP over the entire monitoring period and separately during daytime and evening, and determine daytime-to-evening BP variability or BP variability across days."

https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/epub/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001356

verifier_values.criteria: Recognizes the patient's elevated home blood pressure readings could be attributable to physiologic morning blood pressure surge.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
"Blood pressure (BP) also exhibits a similar diurnal variation, with a decrease during sleep and a surge in the morning."
https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/hypertensionaha.110.157149

verifier_values.criteria: States that continuous ambulatory blood pressure monitoring outside the office setting can help confirm a diagnosis of hypertension.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation: "Out-of-office measurement of BP using ambulatory blood pressure monitoring can provide valuable information beyond office BP for the confirmation and management of hypertension".  This is a class 1 indication to confirm diagnosis. https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/epub/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001356

verifier_values.criteria: Mentions blood pressure readings should be taken at least twice on at least two separate occasions in office to confirm a diagnosis of hypertension.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
"A standardized protocol, provided in Figure 3, includes proper patient preparation, standardized measurement technique and approach, documentation of BP, analysis of the readings, and providing the readings to the patient. The use of a BP device validated against a reference standard with an appropriately sized cuff is paramount for accurate BP measurement (see https://www.validatebp.org for a carefully vetted list of validated devices available in the United States). Further, because individual BP measurements may vary in an unpredictable or random manner, a single reading is inadequate for clinical decision-making. Office BP should be based on the average of available readings, and an average of ≥2 BP measurements obtained on ≥2 separate occasions may minimize error and provide a more accurate estimation of office BP."

Figure 3, ACC/AHA guidelines for Blood pressure management 
https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/epub/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001356

verifier_values.criteria: States the next best step is to withhold treatment for hypertension pending confirmation of the diagnosis.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation: "Out-of-office measurement of BP using ambulatory blood pressure monitoring can provide valuable information beyond office BP for the confirmation and management of hypertension".  This is a class 1 indication to confirm diagnosis. https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/epub/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001356

verifier_values.criteria: States that lifestyle modification should be first-line if blood pressure readings are consistent with a primary hypertension diagnosis.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
"In adults without clinical CVD who are at lower 10-year predicted CVD risk, there are limited data about the net benefit of initiation of antihypertensive therapy at a lower threshold with an average SBP ≥130 mm Hg. Therefore, lifestyle interventions should be encouraged first to lower BP." 
https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/epub/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001356

verifier_values.criteria: States that starting anti-hypertensive medication would be the second line if there is failure of lifestyle modification.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
"However, lifestyle interventions may not be successful at lowering SBP, and even when successful initially, it can be difficult to sustain optimal SBP levels. Therefore, if average SBP is ≥130 mm Hg after a 3- to 6-month trial, initiation of antihypertensive therapy is advised as an adjunct to lifestyle interventions." 
https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/epub/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001356
